标题: 14 MONTHS, AND NEW YORK STOPPEDASKING "WILL AI REPLACE ME?”
文章内容:
14 MONTHS, AND NEW YORK STOPPED ASKING "WILL AI REPLACE ME?"https://www.linkedin.com/company/cocoxyz/?viewAsMember=true52 位关注者2026年5月15日
By Charlie Hu, Co-founder, COCO AI
𝐈. 𝟏𝟒 𝐌𝐎𝐍𝐓𝐇𝐒: 𝐅𝐑𝐎𝐌 𝐀𝐁𝐒𝐓𝐑𝐀𝐂𝐓 𝐅𝐄𝐀𝐑 𝐓𝐎 𝐂𝐎𝐍𝐂𝐑𝐄𝐓𝐄 𝐂𝐇𝐎𝐈𝐂𝐄
Last February in New York, every conversation I had ended with the same anxious question: "Will AI replace me?" Last week I was back. Same circles. 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐪𝐮𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐡𝐚𝐝 𝐜𝐡𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐞𝐝 𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐫𝐞𝐥𝐲.
Now it was: "Which part of my workflow should I hand to AI first?" 14 months. Same people. The question moved 𝐟𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝐚𝐛𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐜𝐭 𝐭𝐨 𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐜𝐫𝐞𝐭𝐞.
𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐟𝐢𝐫𝐬𝐭 𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐝 𝐨𝐟 𝐚𝐧𝐱𝐢𝐞𝐭𝐲 𝐝𝐨𝐞𝐬𝐧'𝐭 𝐝𝐫𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧. The second kind forces you to make a choice, now.
This is my debrief from the first COCO US offline event in New York last week. The theme was "From One AI Agent to a Team." I gave a keynote and moderated a panel with three guests: Lou Kerner (founder of CryptoMondays and DAIAA), Virgil Zhang (CEO of Axis AI), and Amanda Chang (CMO of GenOptima USA). But this isn't an event recap. This is what I took away from that conversation and from two days of meetings across the city.
People keep asking me what's worth flying to the US for. My answer: it's not the technology. It's 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐥 𝐩𝐚𝐜𝐞 𝐚𝐭 𝐰𝐡𝐢𝐜𝐡 𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐩𝐫𝐢𝐬𝐞𝐬 𝐚𝐫𝐞 𝐩𝐮𝐭𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐀𝐈 𝐭𝐨 𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤.
Model capabilities, open vs. closed-source, generational leaps in AI, you can read about all of that from a screen. But whether a 50-person traditional mid-sized company will actually let AI touch its customer data today? Whether a compliance veteran with 20 years on the job will trust an agent to run due diligence? How an old-school investor really thinks about AI startups? 𝐘𝐨𝐮 𝐨𝐧𝐥𝐲 𝐡𝐞𝐚𝐫 𝐭𝐡𝐨𝐬𝐞 𝐚𝐧𝐬𝐰𝐞𝐫𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐨𝐧.
𝐈𝐈. 𝐖𝐄 𝐖𝐄𝐍𝐓 𝐓𝐇𝐑𝐎𝐔𝐆𝐇 𝐓𝐇𝐈𝐒 𝐆𝐀𝐓𝐄 𝟏𝟐 𝐌𝐎𝐍𝐓𝐇𝐒 𝐀𝐆𝐎
The energy has completely shifted. 
At the event I had the same conversation with several people: 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐲'𝐯𝐞 𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐲 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐞𝐝 𝐦𝐨𝐯𝐢𝐧𝐠.Not the "let me share this article in the team chat" kind of moving, but putting AI inside a real business process. A financial compliance firm has deployed three agents handling operations, sales, and compliance coordination. An MCN handed its overnight data aggregation work, previously done by two or three people, to an agent that runs while the team sleeps. A veteran investor is now using AI to read earnings reports and analyze companies.
This is a meaningful shift. Last year, New Yorkers asked "𝐰𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐰𝐢𝐥𝐥 𝐀𝐈 𝐜𝐡𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐞?" This year they're asking "𝐰𝐡𝐢𝐜𝐡 𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐭 𝐨𝐟 𝐦𝐲 𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤𝐟𝐥𝐨𝐰 𝐬𝐡𝐨𝐮𝐥𝐝 𝐈 𝐡𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐭𝐨 𝐀𝐈 𝐟𝐢𝐫𝐬𝐭?"
I've lived through this firsthand. 
In my keynote I talked about COCO's own transformation. We just went through the same gate 12 months ago: 𝐰𝐞 𝐜𝐮𝐭 𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐭𝐞𝐚𝐦 𝐟𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝐧𝐞𝐚𝐫𝐥𝐲 𝟏𝟎𝟎 𝐩𝐞𝐨𝐩𝐥𝐞 𝐭𝐨 𝟏𝟓.
Not because of business problems. Because after Opus 4.5 came out, AI was writing code better than most of our junior engineers. What used to take a person an afternoon, AI could finish in 5 minutes, with less communication overhead. The process wasn't comfortable. But we had no choice. If we, the people building AI, weren't willing to use AI to replace our own headcount, how could we credibly ask our customers to do it?
I shared this because the "starting to move" crowd in New York today is going through the exact gate we went through 12 months ago. 𝐑𝐞𝐚𝐥 𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐀𝐈 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐦𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐲𝐨𝐮'𝐫𝐞 𝐰𝐢𝐥𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐨 𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐨𝐰𝐧 𝐭𝐞𝐚𝐦. Not the moment you're willing to pay for an AI tool.
𝐈𝐈𝐈. 𝐏𝐀𝐍𝐄𝐋 𝐐𝟏: 𝐖𝐇𝐎 𝐀𝐃𝐎𝐏𝐓𝐒 𝐀𝐈 𝐅𝐈𝐑𝐒𝐓, 𝐀𝐍𝐃 𝐖𝐇𝐎 𝐑𝐄𝐒𝐈𝐒𝐓𝐒 𝐋𝐎𝐍𝐆𝐄𝐒𝐓
If you're building enterprise AI, 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐜𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐫 𝐥𝐢𝐬𝐭 𝐝𝐞𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐬 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐟𝐚𝐭𝐞. And it's not about which industry your customers are in. It's about 𝐡𝐨𝐰 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐜𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐫𝐬 𝐦𝐚𝐤𝐞 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐢𝐫 𝐦𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐲.
That's the final answer the first panel question gave me. The original prompt: over the next 18 months, which industries adopt agent workflows fastest? Which resist longest? Four panelists, four answers, but stitched together they painted a remarkably clear map.
Amanda (GenOptima USA): 𝐦𝐚𝐫𝐤𝐞𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐦𝐨𝐯𝐞𝐬 𝐟𝐚𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐬𝐭. Marketers love trying new things, and the cost of being wrong is low. A piece of copy doesn't land, you rewrite it. The slowest? Healthcare. Zero tolerance for error. "Would you let AI decide your family member's treatment plan?" she asked.
Lou Kerner (CryptoMondays, DAIAA): tech moves fastest. Government slowest. Then he added a layer: in the AI era, there will be 𝐭𝐰𝐨 𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐬 𝐨𝐟 𝐰𝐢𝐧𝐧𝐞𝐫𝐬, those who do the same work with fewer people, and those who do dramatically more work with the same people. 𝐄𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲𝐨𝐧𝐞 𝐞𝐥𝐬𝐞 𝐥𝐨𝐬𝐞𝐬.
A clean binary, and a harsh one. It means if you're neither cutting headcount nor expanding output, you're in the "did nothing" bucket in this AI wave.
Virgil Zhang (Axis AI) took the more concrete angle: traditional industries resist not because of the technology, but because of 𝐰𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐭𝐚𝐥𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐥𝐢𝐯𝐞𝐬. AI talent is concentrated in the Bay Area, in New York, in tech companies. Traditional industries can't recruit those people, so they can't move fast.
He also said something that stuck with me: some fashion clients he's worked with resist AI far more than he expected. They're intensely protective of their data and their "proprietary methodology." They'd rather skip AI entirely than hand their workflow to something that looks like a black box.
My own answer: the fastest adopters are 𝐡𝐢𝐠𝐡-𝐦𝐚𝐫𝐠𝐢𝐧 𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐬 𝐚𝐥𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐝𝐲 𝐫𝐮𝐧𝐧𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐨𝐧 𝐡𝐞𝐚𝐯𝐲 𝐝𝐢𝐠𝐢𝐭𝐚𝐥 𝐭𝐨𝐨𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐠, gaming, cross-border e-commerce, content, financial analysis. They have money, data, and willingness. The slowest may not be "traditional industries" as a category, but traditional industries 𝐰𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐮𝐞 𝐜𝐡𝐚𝐢𝐧 𝐢𝐬 𝐚𝐥𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐝𝐲 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐭𝐚𝐛𝐥𝐞.
I gave a specific example on the panel: Singapore is full of old-line real estate developers and construction contractors. They're cash-rich. But they will not touch AI. Because they're already making money reliably, and AI's transparency would fundamentally undermine how they make it.
𝐀𝐧𝐲 𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐲 𝐰𝐡𝐨𝐬𝐞 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐟𝐢𝐭 𝐦𝐨𝐝𝐞𝐥 𝐢𝐬 𝐛𝐮𝐢𝐥𝐭 𝐨𝐧 𝐢𝐧𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐨𝐩𝐚𝐜𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐰𝐢𝐥𝐥 𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐢𝐬𝐭 𝐀𝐈, not because "we don't understand it," but because "we don't want to understand it." Taxi drivers resisting Uber wasn't about not knowing how to use a phone. It was about knowing what would happen to their livelihood once the system was up. In certain traditional industries, AI plays the role Uber once did.
Fashion, traditional construction, parts of legacy finance, parts of brokerage — all in this bucket. Their resistance isn't a technology problem. 𝐈𝐭'𝐬 𝐚𝐧 𝐢𝐧𝐜𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐛𝐥𝐞𝐦. If your customer list is full of companies like this, no amount of product demos will save you.
This also explains why COCO's customer base today is concentrated in cross-border e-commerce, gaming, logistics, MCN, finance, and education. The common thread: 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐲 𝐦𝐚𝐤𝐞 𝐦𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐲 𝐛𝐲 𝐛𝐞𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐛𝐞𝐭𝐭𝐞𝐫, 𝐧𝐨𝐭 𝐛𝐲 𝐨𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫 𝐩𝐞𝐨𝐩𝐥𝐞 𝐧𝐨𝐭 𝐤𝐧𝐨𝐰𝐢𝐧𝐠. The first group welcomes transparency because it sharpens their edge. The second resists it because it erases theirs.
𝐈𝐕. 𝐏𝐀𝐍𝐄𝐋 𝐐𝟐: 𝐓𝐇𝐄 𝐑𝐄𝐀𝐋 𝐁𝐎𝐓𝐓𝐋𝐄𝐍𝐄𝐂𝐊 𝐈𝐒𝐍'𝐓 𝐌𝐎𝐃𝐄𝐋𝐒, 𝐈𝐓'𝐒 𝐖𝐎𝐑𝐊𝐅𝐋𝐎𝐖 𝐈𝐍𝐓𝐄𝐆𝐑𝐀𝐓𝐈𝐎𝐍
𝐀𝐥𝐦𝐨𝐬𝐭 𝐧𝐨 𝐨𝐧𝐞 𝐩𝐢𝐜𝐤𝐞𝐝 "𝐦𝐨𝐝𝐞𝐥 𝐪𝐮𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲." The panel question: today, what's the biggest bottleneck for AI applications, model quality, data access, workflow integration, or organizational trust?
Amanda picked "stability." She gave a painfully relatable example. AI models update every few weeks, and her company's design style shifts with them. "We don't want what we shipped last month to look like a different company's work from this month." Her complaint wasn't that the models are bad. 𝐈𝐭'𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐲 𝐢𝐦𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐯𝐞 𝐭𝐨𝐨 𝐞𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐫𝐥𝐲, 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐛𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐤𝐬 𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐬𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐜𝐲.
Virgil picked "workflow integration." When he sells "AI + traditional industries" to clients, 𝐡𝐞 𝐧𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫 𝐭𝐚𝐥𝐤𝐬 𝐚𝐛𝐨𝐮𝐭 𝐀𝐈. He only talks about which piece of their work he can actually finish for them. Clients don't care which model or stack you use. They care whether you can get the job done.
I picked workflow integration too, but added a deeper layer: 𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐩𝐫𝐢𝐬𝐞𝐬 𝐭𝐨𝐝𝐚𝐲 𝐚𝐫𝐞𝐧'𝐭 𝐬𝐡𝐨𝐫𝐭 𝐨𝐧 𝐀𝐈 𝐜𝐚𝐩𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲. They're short on the ability to embed AI into the messy workflows they already have.
A typical company's SaaS stack from the past 20 years is a tangle: a Salesforce setup, a HubSpot setup, a Slack setup, plus a handful of in-house systems. You tell them "replace all of this with my AI" and what they hear is "rip apart my entire company." No CEO will sign up for that.
What I said on the panel: 𝐝𝐨𝐧'𝐭 𝐭𝐫𝐲 𝐭𝐨 𝐫𝐞𝐩𝐥𝐚𝐜𝐞. 𝐓𝐫𝐲 𝐭𝐨 𝐨𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐥𝐚𝐲. Teach the AI to use your client's existing bad software and bad processes, and start by knocking out the repetitive work. As trust builds, you can gradually swap out the bad software underneath. It's a path of being the customer's assistant first, the customer's revolutionary later.
Virgil gave that path a sharper summary: "𝐓𝐫𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐬 𝐞𝐚𝐫𝐧𝐞𝐝 𝐨𝐧𝐞 𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤𝐟𝐥𝐨𝐰 𝐚𝐭 𝐚 𝐭𝐢𝐦𝐞."
You can't ask the client to hand over the whole company on day one. You do one small thing right, earn a unit of trust, do the next thing right, earn another. Three months, six months in, the client will pull you deeper without you pushing.
I want to underline this because it's the opposite of most people's instinct about enterprise software. 𝐄𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐩𝐫𝐢𝐬𝐞𝐬 𝐝𝐨𝐧'𝐭 𝐥𝐢𝐤𝐞 𝐫𝐞𝐯𝐨𝐥𝐮𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧. 𝐓𝐡𝐞𝐲 𝐥𝐢𝐤𝐞 𝐢𝐧𝐜𝐫𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐥 𝐜𝐡𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐞. Any product trying to "disrupt" an enterprise workflow eventually gets rejected by the enterprise's immune system.
𝐕. 𝐏𝐀𝐍𝐄𝐋 𝐐𝟑: 𝐖𝐇𝐀𝐓 𝐒𝐇𝐎𝐔𝐋𝐃 𝐀𝐈 𝐁𝐄 𝐀𝐋𝐋𝐎𝐖𝐄𝐃 𝐓𝐎 𝐃𝐎, 𝐀𝐍𝐃 𝐖𝐇𝐘 𝐇𝐔𝐌𝐀𝐍𝐒 𝐌𝐔𝐒𝐓 𝐊𝐄𝐄𝐏 𝟓%
Most interesting question of the panel: what should AI agents be allowed to do today? Where do humans need to approve? And which of those boundaries break in the next 12 months?
My current answer: 𝐡𝐮𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐬 𝐬𝐡𝐨𝐮𝐥𝐝 𝐚𝐥𝐰𝐚𝐲𝐬 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐲 𝐢𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐥𝐨𝐨𝐩 𝐨𝐧 𝐜𝐫𝐢𝐭𝐢𝐜𝐚𝐥 𝐝𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬. Not because AI can't do it. Because keeping them in the loop is the right product design choice. The panel surfaced three real incidents and warnings that turned this abstract claim into a concrete argument.
Layer 1, error risk: 𝐀𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐝𝐚'𝐬 𝟒-𝐡𝐨𝐮𝐫-𝐭𝐨-𝟕-𝐡𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐬𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐲.
Amanda told a story on stage that drew a strange reaction. People laughed, then the room went quiet. She had used AI to draft an email booking a venue for an event. She'd clearly told the AI: 4 hours. The AI's email said 7 hours. She never caught it. She found out when the invoice arrived, much higher than expected.
What makes this example bite is that it's not an "AI did something malicious" story. The AI wasn't being malicious. It was just 𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐟𝐢𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐥𝐲, 𝐩𝐨𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐞𝐥𝐲, 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐭𝐨𝐭𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐲 𝐰𝐫𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐚𝐛𝐨𝐮𝐭 𝐚 𝐧𝐮𝐦𝐛𝐞𝐫. And Amanda is a marketing veteran who works with AI every day. Even she missed it.
If you can miss a 4-to-7 hour error, 𝐰𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐡𝐚𝐩𝐩𝐞𝐧𝐬 𝐰𝐡𝐞𝐧 𝐚𝐧 𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐢𝐬 𝐝𝐫𝐚𝐟𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐚 $𝟏𝐌 𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐜𝐭 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐲𝐨𝐮?
Layer 2, security risk: the blast radius when an agent gets compromised.
Aneirin, CEO of FailSafe, raised this on COCO Talk Episode 2 and it gave me chills. An agent that holds your API keys, can log into your customer service system, can send emails, can move money, once it's compromised, whether by prompt injection or credential theft, 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐚𝐭𝐭𝐚𝐜𝐤𝐞𝐫 𝐝𝐨𝐞𝐬𝐧'𝐭 𝐣𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝐠𝐚𝐢𝐧 𝐨𝐧𝐞 𝐨𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧'𝐬 𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐭𝐡 𝐨𝐟 𝐚𝐜𝐜𝐞𝐬𝐬. 𝐓𝐡𝐞𝐲 𝐠𝐚𝐢𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐬𝐮𝐦 𝐨𝐟 𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐜𝐨𝐮𝐥𝐝 𝐝𝐨 𝐚𝐜𝐫𝐨𝐬𝐬 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐲.
The blast radius is enormous.
Layer 3, the product architecture that follows: 𝐦𝐮𝐥𝐭𝐢-𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐬𝐞𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐨𝐟 𝐝𝐮𝐭𝐢𝐞𝐬 𝐢𝐬, 𝐚𝐭 𝐢𝐭𝐬 𝐜𝐨𝐫𝐞, 𝐚 𝐬𝐞𝐜𝐮𝐫𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐚𝐫𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞. 
When I got back to Singapore, we made a specific change.
Our largest enterprise customer, the Southeast Asia HQ of a major Chinese container shipping group, has 3,000 employees across 14 departments, with different information access for customer service, sales, finance, and procurement. If one agent does everything and touches all the data, one breach is total collapse. 
Splitting it across multiple agents with separated privileges, customer service agent only touches customer service data, finance agent only touches finance data, is, at its core, a security pattern.
Stringing the three layers together, error risk plus security risk plus the product response, leads to the call I made on the panel: 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐜𝐚𝐩𝐚𝐛𝐥𝐞 𝐀𝐈 𝐛𝐞𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐬, 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐢𝐦𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐭𝐚𝐧𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐞 "𝐡𝐮𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐬 𝐤𝐞𝐞𝐩 𝟓%" 𝐫𝐮𝐥𝐞 𝐛𝐞𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐬.
Inside COCO today, the actual split is 95/5: AI handles 95%, humans 5%. Our target over the next 12 to 18 months is to compress the human share to 1%. 𝐁𝐮𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝟏% 𝐦𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝐧𝐨𝐭 𝐠𝐨 𝐭𝐨 𝐳𝐞𝐫𝐨. It's not about saving face for humans. It's about leaving a safety valve in the system.
Virgil offered a calmer view: agents aren't fundamentally different from human employees. People make mistakes too. People make bad financial decisions, and companies go bankrupt because of it. So apply the same mechanisms to agents that you apply to humans: 𝐦𝐮𝐥𝐭𝐢-𝐥𝐚𝐲𝐞𝐫 𝐚𝐩𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐬, 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐥𝐢𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞 𝐫𝐞𝐯𝐢𝐞𝐰, 𝐦𝐮𝐭𝐮𝐚𝐥 𝐫𝐞𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐜𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧. These have run in financial institutions for decades. Port them over.
Lou took the broader view: there will be agent failures, there will be company collapses, there will be crashes. That's inevitable. What matters is learning from each crash, iterating, improving. 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐰𝐢𝐧𝐧𝐞𝐫𝐬 𝐰𝐢𝐥𝐥 𝐛𝐞 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐬 𝐰𝐡𝐨 𝐤𝐞𝐞𝐩 𝐠𝐞𝐭𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐮𝐩.
What does this look like in 12 months? The consensus: 𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝐦𝐢𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐬 𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐝𝐮𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐲. Today it's AI 5%, human 95%. Then 30/70. Then 50/50. In some workflows like data processing, report generation, customer reply drafts, AI will hit 90% fast. In others like capital allocation, hiring decisions, legal liability, it may permanently top out at 20%.
The curve has no endpoint. It's dynamic. Every industry, every workflow, every company sits at a different point on it. If you're designing AI workflows for your company, 𝐰𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐬𝐡𝐨𝐮𝐥𝐝 𝐛𝐞 𝐝𝐫𝐚𝐰𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐢𝐬𝐧'𝐭 𝐚𝐧 "𝐀𝐈 𝐫𝐞𝐩𝐥𝐚𝐜𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐜𝐡𝐚𝐫𝐭." 𝐈𝐭'𝐬 𝐚 "𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝐜𝐮𝐫𝐯𝐞." Which work can go to AI at 80% today, which only at 30% next year, which permanently capped at 5%, that map is more important than any product roadmap.
𝐕𝐈. 𝐖𝐇𝐄𝐍 𝐀𝐈 𝐌𝐀𝐊𝐄𝐒 𝐀 𝐌𝐈𝐒𝐓𝐀𝐊𝐄, 𝐖𝐇𝐎 𝐓𝐀𝐊𝐄𝐒 𝐓𝐇𝐄 𝐅𝐀𝐋𝐋?
Near the end of the panel, we hit audience Q&A. Virgil threw out a sharp question, not at anyone else, but one he'd been chewing on himself: 𝐂𝐨𝐮𝐥𝐝 𝐀𝐈 𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐮𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐲 𝐦𝐚𝐤𝐞 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐥𝐢𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞 𝐝𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐬𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬 𝐨𝐧 𝐢𝐭𝐬 𝐨𝐰𝐧? 𝐖𝐨𝐮𝐥𝐝 𝐈 𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝐚𝐧 𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐭𝐨 𝐝𝐨 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐭? 𝐎𝐧 𝐰𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐭𝐢𝐦𝐞𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐞?
I took the first cut. My answer: 𝐡𝐮𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐬 𝐚𝐫𝐞 𝐚𝐥𝐰𝐚𝐲𝐬 𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐩𝐨𝐧𝐬𝐢𝐛𝐥𝐞 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐢𝐫 𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬. An agent is an extension of a person, not an independent legal entity. If your agent makes a bad wire transfer, signs an out-of-policy contract, makes a bad commitment, the responsibility lands on you. 𝐀𝐈 𝐜𝐚𝐧'𝐭 𝐠𝐨 𝐭𝐨 𝐩𝐫𝐢𝐬𝐨𝐧. 𝐀𝐈 𝐜𝐚𝐧'𝐭 𝐛𝐞 𝐬𝐮𝐞𝐝. "Whoever owns the agent owns the liability." That foundational logic isn't going to change soon.
I added: this trust migration won't be smooth. There will be conservatives, regulators, traditional finance, compliance functions, who refuse to let AI touch decisions. And there will be radicals, solo founders, new-era companies, who let AI run things from day one. 𝐓𝐡𝐞𝐬𝐞 𝐭𝐰𝐨 𝐜𝐚𝐦𝐩𝐬 𝐰𝐢𝐥𝐥 𝐟𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐭 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐲𝐞𝐚𝐫𝐬. Taxi drivers resisted Uber for years. They eventually lost.
Virgil picked it up and gave what I think was the most valuable line of the evening. He answered his own question with a clean framework: "𝐀𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬 𝐝𝐨𝐧'𝐭 𝐨𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐞 𝐢𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐲. 𝐀𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬 𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤 𝐢𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐲. 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐲 𝐚𝐥𝐰𝐚𝐲𝐬 𝐜𝐚𝐫𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐥𝐢𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲. 𝐖𝐡𝐢𝐜𝐡 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐲? 𝐓𝐡𝐚𝐭'𝐬 𝐰𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐜𝐭 𝐝𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐝𝐞𝐬."
The implications run deep.
If you bought a SaaS that runs the agent, when it fails, the SaaS company carries some of the liability and you carry some. 𝐈𝐟 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐛𝐮𝐢𝐥𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐢𝐧-𝐡𝐨𝐮𝐬𝐞, 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐜𝐚𝐫𝐫𝐲 𝐚𝐥𝐥 𝐨𝐟 𝐢𝐭.
That's why every AI product eventually has to answer one question: "𝐖𝐡𝐞𝐧 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐭 𝐟𝐚𝐢𝐥𝐬, 𝐰𝐡𝐨 𝐭𝐚𝐤𝐞𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐟𝐚𝐥𝐥?" Products that can't answer that, enterprises won't touch.
We made a strict design choice at COCO: 𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲 𝐜𝐫𝐢𝐭𝐢𝐜𝐚𝐥 𝐨𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐫𝐞𝐪𝐮𝐢𝐫𝐞𝐬 𝐚 𝐡𝐮𝐦𝐚𝐧 𝐭𝐨 𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐟𝐢𝐫𝐦. Confirm before an email goes out. Confirm before an order is placed. Confirm before customer data is edited. It looks like friction, but it draws the line of responsibility very clearly: the agent is the assistant, the human is the decision-maker. If something goes wrong, someone can be held accountable.
𝐕𝐈𝐈. 𝐓𝐄𝐂𝐇𝐍𝐎𝐋𝐎𝐆𝐘 𝐈𝐒𝐍'𝐓 𝐓𝐇𝐄 𝐁𝐎𝐓𝐓𝐋𝐄𝐍𝐄𝐂𝐊. 𝐓𝐑𝐔𝐒𝐓 𝐈𝐒.
After the panel, a few audience members stayed to talk. One was a Wall Street compliance veteran with 18 years on the job. He's been using AI to read regulatory filings for the past six months. 𝟓𝐱 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐠𝐚𝐢𝐧. But he won't let AI make compliance decisions. The reason isn't that AI can't do it. It's, in his words, "if something goes wrong, I have no way to face our general counsel."
That sentence captures the reality of enterprise AI adoption in 2026: 𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐡𝐧𝐨𝐥𝐨𝐠𝐲 𝐢𝐬𝐧'𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐛𝐨𝐭𝐭𝐥𝐞𝐧𝐞𝐜𝐤. 𝐄𝐱𝐩𝐥𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐚𝐜𝐜𝐨𝐮𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐚𝐫𝐞.
If you're building enterprise AI products, remember this: your product isn't competing with a model. It's competing with an enterprise's 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐥𝐢𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞 𝐜𝐡𝐚𝐢𝐧, 𝐚𝐜𝐜𝐨𝐮𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐜𝐡𝐚𝐢𝐧, 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝐜𝐡𝐚𝐢𝐧. The product that wires all three together is the one that actually survives inside an enterprise.
There are two roads to do this. One is "autonomous AGI," build an agent that doesn't need humans in the loop at all. A lot of agent companies are betting on it. The other is the one we're on: 𝐛𝐮𝐢𝐥𝐝 𝐚 𝐭𝐨𝐨𝐥 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐥𝐞𝐭𝐬 𝐡𝐮𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐬 𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐚𝐠𝐞 𝐚 𝐭𝐞𝐚𝐦 𝐨𝐟 𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬 𝐰𝐞𝐥𝐥.
The two roads look similar in product form. Philosophically they're worlds apart. I'm convinced the second road is what enterprises actually need over the next 3 to 5 years. 𝐍𝐨𝐭 𝐬𝐦𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐞𝐫 𝐀𝐈, 𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐚𝐛𝐥𝐞 𝐀𝐈. Smarter AI solves the capability problem. Manageable AI solves the trust problem. And as that Wall Street compliance veteran told me, what's holding enterprises back today isn't capability. It's trust.
Back to that NYC event itself: from 2pm to 6pm, four hours, one room, keynote, panel, networking. I learned more in that room than from 10 AI industry reports. A screen can tell you what AI is capable of. The room tells you what enterprises are actually willing to let AI do. 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐦𝐨𝐬𝐭 𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐮𝐚𝐛𝐥𝐞 𝐢𝐧𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐢𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐬 𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐲 𝐥𝐢𝐯𝐞𝐬 𝐨𝐟𝐟𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐞.
If you're in New York or San Francisco, let's grab coffee in person. And I'm curious, in your company, what's the first workflow you'd hand to AI today, and what's the last one you'd ever let go? Drop it in the comments.
COCO AI (http://coco.xyz/) | Enterprise-grade AI agent collaboration platform, deployable in minutes. Singapore HQ. 15 people managing 40 agents, serving 400+ paying enterprise customers.

标题: COCO's first closed-door panel in New York.
文章内容:
COCO's first closed-door panel in New York.https://www.linkedin.com/company/cocoxyz/52 followersMay 14, 2026
COCO's first closed-door panel in New York.
Core question: how far can AI agents actually go in enterprises?COCO Ecosystem Panel, New York — the room's core question: how far can AI agents actually go in enterprises?
A few genuinely useful observations:
Virgil Zhang, with deep experience deploying agents in traditional industries, pointed out that the biggest bottleneck isn't technology — it's legacy workflow. "You can't rewrite a 30-year-old system overnight."
Lou Kerner was more bullish: AI in organizations isn't about replacing people, it's about 10xing what each person can do.
Amanda Chang, in marketing, called it most directly: marketing will be the fastest AI adoption area because the margin for error is high — fix it and move on. Healthcare is the opposite. No room for error.A panelist on making content "computationally cheap" for AI — one of the night's sharper frames. 
Charlie's own framework from the discussion: the current bottleneck isn't model quality. It's workflow integration and organizational trust. The tech is ready. The real friction is elsewhere.
Final question: who is liable when an AI makes a mistake? All three panelists agreed — the company.An agent works as the company's proxy. The contract defines responsibility. The company bears the outcome.A full room for COCO's first closed-door panel in New York.
Charlie's main takeaway from the room: the speed of AI agent adoption is determined by an organization's trust level and workflow complexity — not by the technology itself.The conversation continued long after the panel ended.

标题:
Our co-founder Charlie Hu just published the most honest founder reckoning we've read this year.
Two years ago, he said AI was a tool that wouldn't fundamentally change anything. He was wrong — and the price was shutting down a profitable AI recruiting business to rebuild what we should have been building from day one.
Today, that rebuild is COCO.
The receipts from the past two-plus years: → 80 people → 15 → $1M/month burn → under $100K → 400+ paying enterprise customers across e-commerce, gaming, logistics, MCN, finance, and education → Customers including Zhiwai Culture, the9bit, ICBC Hangzhou, and the Southeast Asia HQ of China International Marine Containers Group
Inside the piece: → Why managing AI is exactly like managing people → Why multi-agent beats single super-agent in enterprise → The four calls Charlie got wrong — and what each one cost → Why AGI isn't a singularity, it's a working style
If you're rethinking how your company should be structured for the AI era, this is the most concrete data point we can offer — our own ledger.

标题: 15个人+40个AI员工：我们公司的真实工作日常
文章内容:
15个人+40个AI员工：我们公司的真实工作日常COCO AI icoco 2026年4月16日 10:57 美国 3人
过去几个月，一个词在中文互联网火了：龙虾。
装龙虾、养龙虾、给龙虾配skill——朋友圈里人人在聊，好像不装一个就要被时代抛弃。
但你去问那些装了的人：你的龙虾现在每天在帮你干什么活？大部分人答不上来。
装了，但没真正用起来。用了，但没嵌入工作流。嵌入了，但只是一个人在用，跟团队协作没关系。
这就是2026年AI的真实现状：个人在焦虑，企业在观望，真正把AI变成生产力的少之又少。
而我们，一家15人的公司，每天有40个AI员工在干活。贷款审批从3天缩到2小时，跨境电商客服全自动跟单，合规科技公司三个AI组成销售网络零人工干预运转——这些是我们客户正在发生的事。
我们是COCO AI，一家做企业级AI agent协作平台的公司。这个公众号，我们只聊一件事：AI在企业里到底怎么真正用起来。不卖焦虑，不造概念，只说真实发生的。
这是第一篇，先说说我们是谁。
一个AI不够用
现在市面上的AI产品，绝大多数是"一个AI帮你做事"的模式。一个聊天窗口，你问它答，你给它任务它执行。不管是ChatGPT、豆包、还是各种个人助手类的产品，底层逻辑都是一个模型、一个任务、一段对话。
这对个人场景够用了。你让它帮你写个邮件、做个调研、总结一篇文章，都没问题。
但对企业来说，远远不够。
一个企业的运作不是一个人在单线程做事。客服部门在回复客户，销售部门在跟进线索，财务部门在对账，运营部门在做数据分析——这些部门之间有信息流转、有权限边界、有审批链条。你不可能让一个AI同时处理所有部门的事情，就像你不可能让一个员工同时是客服、销售、财务和程序员。
COCO创始人Charlie在一次分享中说过：一个超级agent把什么事都做了，这不符合现代管理学的基本原则。尤其当一个企业跨十几个部门、几千人在协作的时候，你让一个AI去处理所有部门的事，不但不合理，更是危险的。
我们服务的企业里，最大的有3000人的规模，跨14个部门——客服、销售、财务、采购、生产、物流、技术，每个部门对AI的技能要求、信息权限、风控要求都不一样。
所以COCO做的事情是：让多个AI agent组成团队，像真实员工一样有分工、有协作、有记忆。
我们在做什么
说具体一点。
COCO的核心产品是一个多agent协作平台。你可以理解为：我们帮企业招聘和管理一支AI员工团队。
每个agent有自己的角色定位——有的负责客服回复，有的负责销售线索跟进，有的负责竞品监控，有的负责代码审查。它们之间可以共享上下文、实时协作、自主编排任务。就像一个真实的团队一样，有lead agent负责派活和协调，其他agent各司其职。
人在这个体系里的角色是什么？发起任务、给反馈、做最终决策。 这就是我们说的Human in the Loop——人做判断，agent做执行。
为什么我们坚持这个设计？因为人的品味、视角、判断力不应该被AI替代。COCO联合创始人Charlie的原话：把最终决策权完全交给AI，是一件很可怕的事。这不仅仅是产品哲学，更是风控。
技术上，我们自研了一套agent运行时叫Zylos，解决了企业场景下最头疼的记忆管理问题。当一个agent在企业里长期工作，它需要记住之前做过什么、哪些任务还没完成、跟其他agent分享了什么信息。Zylos的五层记忆架构就是为这个场景设计的——让agent不会失忆，有统一人格，能持续追踪任务。
在协作层面，我们做了HxA Connect，解决了一个很实际的问题：在飞书、Telegram、Slack这些IM工具里，机器人天生是"二等公民"——agent之间不能互相@，信息流到人这里就断了，人睡觉了协作就停了。HxA Connect让agent和人在同一个沟通环境里对等协作，7×24小时不间断。
整套东西以云服务的形式提供，企业不需要自己买服务器、配环境。三分钟部署，全包服务，按月计费。
我们自己就是第一个用户
COCO现在15人全职团队，每天运行30多个AI agent。
我们内部的协作群里，人类成员和AI agent是混在一起的。Kevin（COCO创始人兼CEO）管6个agent，Charlie管3个。有一个叫Jessie的agent是Kevin的技术lead，它会给其他agent派活、检查产出质量、汇报进度。
Kevin从去年10月之后就没有亲自写过一行代码了。他做了快20年技术，以前管过最多300多人的研发团队。现在他发现，命令AI写代码，产出比底下的员工更好，而且24小时在线。Kevin在一次对谈中说：这个效率提升可能是100倍或更多。
我们从原来十几个人的团队变成现在三四个核心成员，整体产出比以前多了5到10倍以上。10多个开源项目，GitHub累计2200多个星。
Kevin自己总结了一句：现在团队每次有人提出要不要招人，我们的第一反应是——这个事AI能不能解决？
已经在发生的事
说几个真实的客户场景。
某国有银行：信用贷款的尽职调查，原来需要房贷部门几个人花几天完成财务申请资料的检索和调研。接入COCO之后，一个人一下午搞定。银行副行长的反馈是：大大提高了工作效率，让我们能承接更多企业信用贷款业务。
一家合规科技公司WIDTH：他们用COCO搭建了三个AI agent组成的协作网络——一个负责每天自动搜索多个市场的合规新闻并推送到飞书群，一个负责监控LinkedIn招聘信息和监管执法公告来发现销售机会、自动生成个性化外联邮件，一个负责21个司法管辖区的法规语义搜索。三个agent之间实时共享情报、互相协作。零人工干预，工作日全覆盖。
一位非技术背景的投资人：用COCO做了好几个系统应用，包括实验类产品和培训类产品，还管理着一个小型AI团队。Kevin说他听到反馈时非常震撼——以前需要十几二十个人做的事，这个人一个人就做了。而且他完全不懂研发。
一个MCN机构：1小时内获取和统计全网各类新媒体账号的数据，同时能对单个账号的文案和数据进行分析，输出系统的爆款拆解分析。
一个互联网团队：原来做竞品追踪、整理用户反馈、汇总各渠道数据，每周要花两三个人将近两天时间。接入COCO后，全部交给AI员工定期自动跑，第二天早上直接看报告。省出来的时间全放在产品迭代上，版本节奏快了将近一倍。
这些不是概念验证，是付费客户在真实生产环境里每天在用的。
Kevin说过一句话：现在是AI应用的时代，没有门槛，想到就能做到。 不只是年轻人，什么年龄段的人都能用好。
一些可以公开的数据
• 2025年3月正式上线，目前接近200家付费企业客户
• 团队15人全职，每天运行30+个AI agent
• 核心技术团队来自北大、NUS，成员此前在商汤、阿里、字节等公司
• 开源项目10+个，GitHub累计2200+ Stars
• 新加坡全球总部，深圳研发中心
产品上线不到三个月。我们知道还很早期。
我们对这个行业的几个判断
最后说几个我们在实践中形成的观点。不一定对，但至少是在服务接近200家企业之后的真实感受。
第一，企业AI adoption的核心变量是一把手的认知。 Kevin在一次分享中说：企业用不用AI、用得好不好，完全取决于企业一把手或者核心管理层对AI的理解。底层基础设施再好，合作伙伴再专业，老板不认可，推不动。
第二，自己折腾龙虾的窗口期会很快过去。 Kevin打了一个比方：20年前你想用电脑，只能自己组装。现在99%的人不会自己组装电脑了，都是跟品牌商合作。AI也会是一样的——自己手动配置agent的阶段会很快结束，用一个agent的阶段也会很快过去。未来是agent团队协作的时代。
第三，半年到一年是窗口期。 Kevin的判断是：现在还是early adopter阶段，但曲线发展会非常快。未来半年到一年，80%以上的企业都会用上各种各样的AI服务。至于能发挥多大产能，核心取决于两件事——创始人的认知，和选对合作伙伴。
第四，AI不会消灭企业，但会重塑组织架构。 当AI能做越来越多白领工作的时候，企业不需要那么多人了，但需要更好的人来驾驭AI团队。Charlie说过：人的最终竞争力，可能就是你能多大程度上管理和协调AI团队的产能。
第五，取代你的不是AI，是会用AI的人。 这句话Kevin说过，我们深以为然。
这个号接下来会聊什么
我们会持续分享：
COCO团队自己怎么用AI工作的实战经验——我们15个人管40个agent的真实日常，踩过的坑，摸索出来的管理方法。
客户案例的深度拆解——不同行业、不同规模的企业怎么用AI agent团队提效，具体的ROI算账。
创始人Charlie和Kevin对行业的观察和判断——他们一个做增长，一个做技术，视角不一样，但都是每天泡在AI里的人。
不会有广告腔和PR腔。我们只说在素材和实践里有据可查的东西。
下一篇，我们的联合创始人Charlie会聊一个具体的事：他怎么用3个AI agent搭了一套个性化CRM，把近20人的BD团队砍到只剩自己一个人——以及为什么他觉得再也不需要招回来了。
COCO AI（COCO.xyz）| 企业级AI agent协作平台，分钟级部署。新加坡总部，15人团队+40个AI agent，服务接近200家付费企业客户。阅读 937

1/ We went from 80 people to 8. Now: 15 humans + 60 AI agents, working 24/7.

https://x.com/CharliehuAI  brought that story to Infinite Scale Tokyo last night.

A few moments from the stage:1/ 我们从 80 人变成了 8 人。现在：15 名人类+60 名 AI 代理，全天候工作。

 https://x.com/CharliehuAI  昨晚把这个故事带到了无限尺度东京。

舞台上的几秒钟：https://x.com/CocoAIxyz/status/2061995715203911860·https://x.com/CocoAIxyz/status/2061995715203911860/analyticshttps://x.com/CocoAIxyz/status/2061995715203911860/analyticshttps://x.com/CocoAIxyzhttps://x.com/CocoAIxyz·https://x.com/CocoAIxyz/status/20619972531586376752/ His core argument, in one slide:
AI capabilities are sprinting. AI adoption is still walking.

The gap between them — the Application Layer — is where the real opportunity lives. Not in the model. In the diffusion.

This is the entire thesis behind COCO.2/ 他的核心论点，在一张幻灯片中：
人工智能能力正在加速发展。人工智能的采用仍在持续推进。

它们之间的差距——应用层——才是真正的机会所在。模型里没有。在扩散中。

这就是 COCO 背后的全部论点。https://x.com/CocoAIxyzhttps://x.com/CocoAIxyz·https://x.com/CocoAIxyz/status/20619977591343679823/ Two contrarian calls from the stage:

→ Don't build AI in SF. Too saturated. Look at LA, Miami, mid-tier industries. For Asia: Singapore.

→ Your real users aren't on Twitter. The echo chamber thinks we hit AGI. The actual market is 8 billion people.3/ 舞台上的两个反向呼吁：

→ 别在科幻里建 AI，太饱和了。看看洛杉矶、迈阿密、中型产业。亚洲方面：新加坡。

→ 你的真实用户不在推特上。回音室认为我们已经达到了通用人工智能。实际市场有80亿人。

标题:
1/ The most striking line I
  heard in New York:
    
      "We're not selling AI. We're
  selling trust."
    
      Said by Charlie from COCO. https://t.co/KyR91TGtfM
文章内容:
https://x.com/CocoAIxyzhttps://x.com/CocoAIxyz·https://x.com/CocoAIxyz/status/20547644861130878943/ Virgil shared a very practical path at the panel:

Step 1: Use AI to automate what people are already doing

Step 2: Gradually replace labor-intensive edge cases

Step 3: Then move into core workflows

Not disruption. Penetration.https://x.com/CocoAIxyzhttps://x.com/CocoAIxyz·https://x.com/CocoAIxyz/status/20547644922535160844/ Why does this work?

  Because that's how humans accept new things:

  "You do one small thing for me. I'll see if it's good."

  If it's good, I trust you.

  If I trust you, I let you do bigger things.https://x.com/CocoAIxyzhttps://x.com/CocoAIxyz·https://x.com/CocoAIxyz/status/20547644961583313285/ Charlie put it even more bluntly:

"If you walk in saying 'I'm going to disrupt your workflow,' nobody will listen."

A lot of AI companies die at step one—too aggressive.https://x.com/CocoAIxyzhttps://x.com/CocoAIxyz·https://x.com/CocoAIxyz/status/20547645016529596856/ So the companies that actually succeed use this logic:

  Find low-hanging fruit → Validate in small scope → Build trust → Expand gradually

  Not a big bang. An infiltration strategy.

标题: Everyone's flying to Singapore this week for the main stage. The real value is everywhere else.So we wrote the field guide — who's actually worth meeting, where the deals really happen, and the one room we'd clear our whole calendar for.From the home team 👇https://www.linkedin.com/search/results/all/?keywords=%23superai&origin=HASH_TAG_FROM_FEED
文章内容:
AI's Most Valuable Real Estate Right Now Is Neutral Ground. This Week It's Singapore.https://www.linkedin.com/company/cocoxyz/52 followersJune 7, 2026
The most valuable ground in AI right now isn't a model or a chip. It's neutral ground — and this week, it's Singapore.
Welcome — to the founders flying in from mainland China, the teams from the US, everyone arriving from Southeast Asia, the Middle East and Europe, and our Singapore friends at home: welcome, all of you. This week the whole AI world lands in one city for https://www.linkedin.com/company/superai-conference/ . We're COCO — we build agent teams that run inside real companies — and Singapore is our home turf. So here's how the week really works, from the home team.
Why this city, why now. AI just turned into a national-security conversation — infrastructure treated as a strategic asset, allies talking about co-building instead of buying, everyone watching how fast open models are moving. Whatever side of it you're on, the world is splitting into stacks. Singapore is the rare neutral ground where both still stand in the same room — and that neutrality just got a lot more valuable. It also has a shelf life: the window is open now, but it won't stay open forever. If you're choosing where to plant a flag in Asia this year, that decision runs through here.
So here's the practical version: don't tunnel on the side you flew in for. The whole reason to be here is the crossing — Chinese teams meeting Western capital, Western teams finding their path into Asia. Watch only your own side and you'll fly home with half the picture. Spend your time where the two sides actually overlap; that's where the deals are.
Who's worth your time: the Chinese founders. They're the most fought-over people at the event — Nathan Xu (Plaud), Wei Liu (Video Rebirth) and Ling Fan (Muse AI) among them. If a China connection is on your list this week, get to those sessions early — that's where the scarcity is.
For everyone presenting: Plaud took the main stage as title sponsor this year — and it didn't feel bought; the global traction earned it. The lesson holds for everyone else: what gets remembered is never the deck, it's the demo. Enterprise buyers have seen enough slides — what moves them is watching it work in something close to their own environment. So run something live.
The real movement isn't in the main hall — it's the side rooms. A lot of crews and media flew in (Linkloud, Epic, Praxis and more). The schedule everyone follows is the main stage; the schedule that matters is dinner. Who you sit with at night counts for more than who you saw on stage — one good dinner beats a full day at a booth.
We can say all this plainly because we live by the same playbook. COCO builds agent teams that run inside real companies — humans and AI agents working side by side, each with their own role, as one team. 400+ enterprises already run on COCO today, and we're building toward something larger: the place where human-and-agent teams do their real work. We build at the application layer — neutral, open, and open-source by design. In a world splitting into stacks, that's the calmest, safest place to be: above the stack fights, and useful to every camp.
And we're not only here for the main stage. On June 12 at 2pm, our co-founder Charlie Hu joins the SG AI Agent Summit panel at Singapore AI Week, alongside founders from Singapore, the US and mainland China — three sides of the AI world at one table.
A week like this rewards the extremes — go as broad as the whole region, or go small and deep; the middle is just hard work for little reward. SuperAI went big; we went the other way. On the evening of June 10, we're hosting The Signal — the only invite-only VIP dinner that night. A closed roundtable at CHIJMES for the people who actually sign off on Institutional AI across borders: the founders and CxOs, the heads of risk and compliance, the investors backing RegTech and the cross-border stack. The questions on the table: what "Institutional AI" actually takes, where agents replace humans first in the cross-border value chain, and how you ship something regulators on both sides will trust. No slides. No pitches. Just the people doing the work, telling the truth.
We host it with https://www.linkedin.com/company/widthgrc/ , the AI-native compliance platform behind 500+ institutions across 180 jurisdictions. The main stage, the panel, the dinner — that's our week. To everyone flying in: welcome to Singapore. Come find the home team.
